metaTitle: 5 Things to Know Before Deciding on a Personal Injury Attorney
metaDescription: Not all personal injury attorneys are equal. Before you sign a retainer, know these five things — they are the difference between a great outcome and a costly mistake.
label: Must Read
headline: 5 Things You MUST Know Before Deciding on an Attorney
teaser: Not all personal injury attorneys are equal. Before you sign a retainer, make sure you understand these five things — they're the difference between a great outcome and a frustrating experience.
teaserBullets: How to verify their actual trial record (not just settlements)
What contingency fees really mean for your payout
The one question most people forget to ask
How caseload affects the attention your case gets

content.heading: Verify their trial record — not just their settlements
content.body: Any attorney can settle a case. What matters is whether they're willing — and able — to take a case to trial if necessary. Insurers track which attorneys fold under pressure and lowball accordingly. Ask directly: 'How many personal injury cases have you taken to trial in the last 3 years, and what were the outcomes?'

content.heading: Understand exactly what the contingency fee covers
content.body: Most personal injury attorneys work on a 33–40% contingency, meaning they take that percentage of your settlement. But ask about case expenses: filing fees, expert witnesses, medical record retrieval, deposition costs. Some firms front these and deduct from your settlement; others may charge you win or lose. Get it in writing.

content.heading: Find out who will actually handle your case
content.body: You may meet a senior partner in the consultation but have your case managed entirely by a junior associate or paralegal. Ask directly: 'Who will be my primary point of contact day-to-day?' and 'Will you personally be involved in my case's key decisions?' This matters more than the firm's name on the door.

content.heading: Ask about their current caseload
content.body: An attorney juggling 200–300 active cases has less time for yours. Ask how many active personal injury cases they're currently handling. A focused attorney with 50 cases will often get better results than a busy firm stretched thin across hundreds.

content.heading: Get a realistic timeline for your specific case
content.body: Personal injury cases can take 6 months to several years depending on severity, liability disputes, and whether it goes to trial. Ask for a realistic estimate based on your situation — and ask how often they'll update you on progress. Attorneys who can't answer this question clearly are often the ones who go silent for months.
